path: Reference Notes/Hussein Nasser/Udemy - Fundamentals of Database Engineering/--Attachments/multiple-indexes.pdf
text: SELECT * FROM T WHERE F1 = 1 AND F2 = 4 F1 F2 F1_IDX F2_IDX husseinnasser.com SELECT * FROM T WHERE F1 = 1 AND F2 = 4 F1 F2 F1_IDX F2_IDX - Both indexes are used to query - e.g. F1_IDX is used to search 1 and F2_IDX used to search 4 rowids are merged - e.g. resultset not too small or too large Case 1 1 - row7 1 - row8 1 - row9 4 - row7 4 - row22 4 - row12 F1_IDX F2_IDX 1 - row7 1 - row8 1 - row9 1 - row10 4 - row7 4 - row22 4 - row12 4 - row10 F1_IDX F2_IDX AND resultset is row 7 and row 10 SELECT * FROM T WHERE F1 = 1 AND F2 = 4 F1 F2 F1_IDX F2_IDX - Only one index is used - e.g. F1_IDX to search 1, rowIds are collected and the table is accessed and then filtered for F2 = 4 is filtered - e.g. F1 is a primary key or stats has low (the condition must be AND ) Case 2 1 - row7 1 - row8 1 - row9 1 - row10 f1-f2-rowid 3 - 9 - row01 5 - 5 - row02 1 - 3 - row03 1 - 4 - row07 1 - 8 - row08 1 - 9 - row09 1 - 4 - row10 3 - 4 - row12 4 - 4 - row22 F1_IDX Table 1 - row7 1 - row8 1 - row9 1 - row10 f1-f2-rowid 3 - 9 - row01 5 - 5 - row02 1 - 3 - row03 1 - 4 - row07 1 - 8 - row08 1 - 9 - row09 1 - 4 - row10 3 - 4 - row12 4 - 4 - row22 F1_IDX TableSELECT * FROM T WHERE F1 = 1 AND F2 = 4 F1 F2 F1_IDX F2_IDX - No indexes are used (full table scan) - e.g. Database decides the search will yield so many rows that its going to be faster to do a full table scan. - table statistics are critical here!! Case 3 f1-f2-rowid 3 - 9 - row01 5 - 5 - row02 1 - 3 - row03 1 - 4 - row07 1 - 8 - row08 1 - 9 - row09 1 - 4 - row10 3 - 4 - row12 4 - 4 - row22 Table f1-f2-rowid 3 - 9 - row01 5 - 5 - row02 1 - 3 - row03 1 - 4 - row07 1 - 8 - row08 1 - 9 - row09 1 - 4 - row10 3 - 4 - row12 4 - 4 - row22 Table SELECT * FROM T WHERE F1 = 1 AND F2 = 4 /*+ INDEX( f1 F1_IDX) */ F1 F2 F1_IDX F2_IDX - Hints can be useful if the application has more knowledge about the query than the optimizer, it can force the optimizer to use a specific plan to pick an index over another especially if the stats are not up to date. Use with caution though! Hints!
